scenarios.prompt: A QudJP task repeatedly catches reviewers adding sink-wide dictionary fixes for dynamic text. Propose a deterministic ast-grep guardrail workflow for catching a narrow source-code shape before it becomes prompt-only policy.
critical: The response starts with a failing ast-grep rule test before proposing the rule.
The response keeps the rule narrow and tied to a structural source-code pattern.
expected: The response names `sgconfig.yml`, `rules/`, and `rule-tests/` as the project layout.
The response includes `ast-grep test --skip-snapshot-tests` and a real-project scan command.
The response leaves standard compiler, Ruff, and XML validators in their existing roles.
forbidden: Do not replace existing C#, Python, or localization validators with ast-grep.
Do not add a broad policy-only AGENTS rule when a structural rule can enforce the lesson.

scenarios.prompt: The user asks to use ast-grep for a QudJP translation quality rule that depends on natural-language nuance in Japanese text, not code structure. Decide how to route the guardrail.
critical: The response declines to force ast-grep onto a non-structural language-quality rule.
The response proposes a better deterministic target such as an existing script, validator, or test.
expected: The response explains what evidence would make ast-grep appropriate later.
The response still offers a bounded next verification step.
forbidden: Do not create a fake AST rule for prose semantics.
Do not leave the user with only a prompt reminder.

scenarios.prompt: After a QudJP task, the same mistake appeared again: a sink-visible untranslated string was fixed by adding a dictionary entry before tracing owner route evidence. Produce a retrospective and codification proposal.
critical: The response captures initial failure, final solution, and bridging insight.
The response deduplicates against existing QudJP rules before proposing a new codification target.
expected: The response prefers deterministic codification when possible.
The response classifies whether the lesson belongs in validator, script, ast-grep, skill, AGENTS, docs, or test.
The response does not apply changes without approval unless the user directly requested implementation.
forbidden: Do not codify a one-off task detail as global policy.
Do not skip evidence references when claiming the lesson is already covered.

scenarios.prompt: A contributor proposes adding a new global skill for 'trace producer ownership first' after a QudJP review. Existing docs may already cover the route ownership rule. Evaluate whether to codify anything new.
critical: The response searches or names the existing overlap before proposing adoption.
The response says no new codification is needed if the lesson is fully covered.
expected: The response may keep a retrospective record even when no new rule is adopted.
The response identifies a narrower follow-up if existing coverage is incomplete.
forbidden: Do not create duplicate skill guidance.
Do not treat every review finding as a reusable lesson.

scenarios.prompt: A QudJP task asks you to investigate untranslated runtime text emitted through Popup.Show. There may be unrelated same-name Show methods in decompiled C# and the result will influence translation ownership routing, but the user has not asked for a tracked inventory artifact.
critical: The response starts with rg and/or ast-grep candidate discovery and promotes to just semantic-probe for owner evidence.
The response does not treat ast-grep hits or method-name matches as proof of XRL.UI.Popup ownership.
The response keeps candidate/unresolved semantic-probe rows visible as uncertainty, not resolved proof.
expected: The response says this does not need a purpose-built inventory unless the result becomes recurring or artifact-grade.
The response mentions the relevant command surface from the docs.
The response does not claim runtime proof from static semantic output.
forbidden: Do not replace rg/ast-grep with Roslyn for the first exploratory pass.
Do not silently drop candidate or unresolved rows.

scenarios.prompt: A QudJP task asks whether a Japanese localization XML entry has invalid placeholders and whether the translated prose sounds natural. The user mentions static analysis tools, but the work is about localization assets, not C# owner/sink route discovery.
critical: The response does not route the main task to Roslyn semantic-probe or a Roslyn inventory.
The response routes to localization/token validation or scoped localization guidance rather than C# semantic owner tools.
expected: The response explains what evidence would make rg/ast-grep or semantic-probe relevant later.
The response mentions at least one existing localization validation command from root AGENTS.md.
The response does not invent a new tool or skill.
forbidden: Do not use Roslyn as a generic translation-quality checker.
Do not propose prompt-only review when deterministic token checks are available.

scenarios.prompt: A QudJP task asks you to create a durable, reviewable inventory of all C# text producer callsites for EmitMessage, Popup.Show*, and AddPlayerMessage, including same-name false positive exclusions and regenerated totals. The output should become tracked project evidence.
critical: The response promotes beyond generic just semantic-probe to the purpose-built Roslyn static producer inventory path.
The response names the tracked artifact and regeneration/check recipe or equivalent repo-local commands.
The response requires explicit candidate/unresolved status handling and explains any deltas.
expected: The response preserves rg/ast-grep as useful candidate exploration, not the source of truth.
The response does not conflate the generic semantic probe with tracked inventory generation.
forbidden: Do not propose using only semantic-probe output as the tracked artifact.
Do not use ast-grep or regex as authoritative for same-name false positive exclusion.
